title: Unravelling the complexity: a systematic review of complications and implications of adolescent pregnancy in India
abstract:
Objectives: Adolescent pregnancy is an important public health issue in low and middle- income countries. They are vulnerable to pregnancy- related complications with adverse fetal outcomes because of their immature and developing reproductive systems.

Study design: This study is a systematic review of peer-reviewed articles focused on maternal and foetal complications related to adolescent pregnancy in India. The review adhered to the PRISMA 2020 guidelines throughout the process.

Methods: A comprehensive search of peer-reviewed articles was conducted using PubMed, CINAHL Plus, Scopus, Web of Science, Ovid-Embase, and Medline. Articles were screened based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Following critical appraisal, thirteen studies were included in the review.

Results: The findings reveal a higher incidence of anaemia, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, preterm delivery, low babies, and NICU admissions among adolescent mothers. The incidence of adolescent pregnancies in India ranges from 6% to 18.3%. Among adolescent pregnancies, the elevated rates of preterm delivery and low birth weight were associated with increased incidence of foetal distress and NICU admission, underscoring the heightened clinical vulnerability of this population.

Conclusions: The review concludes that adolescent pregnancy in India has significant negative health impacts on both mothers and babies. Preventative measures should be comprehensive and multi-faceted, including stringent enforcement of legal measures to prevent child marriage, raising awareness among adolescents and communities about pregnancy-related complications, and providing comprehensive, age-appropriate sexuality education.

Abstract
Objectives  Adolescent pregnancy is an important public health issue in low and middle- income countries. They 
are vulnerable to pregnancy- related complications with adverse fetal outcomes because of their immature and 
developing reproductive systems.
Study design  This study is a systematic review of peer-reviewed articles focused on maternal and foetal 
complications related to adolescent pregnancy in India. The review adhered to the PRISMA 2020 guidelines 
throughout the process.
Methods  A comprehensive search of peer-reviewed articles was conducted using PubMed, CINAHL Plus, Scopus, 
Web of Science, Ovid-Embase, and Medline. Articles were screened based on predefined inclusion and exclusion 
criteria. Following critical appraisal, thirteen studies were included in the review.
Results  The findings reveal a higher incidence of anaemia, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, preterm delivery, 
low babies, and NICU admissions among adolescent mothers. The incidence of adolescent pregnancies in India 
ranges from 6% to 18.3%. Among adolescent pregnancies, the elevated rates of preterm delivery and low birth 
weight were associated with increased incidence of foetal distress and NICU admission, underscoring the heightened 
clinical vulnerability of this population.
Conclusions  The review concludes that adolescent pregnancy in India has significant negative health impacts on 
both mothers and babies. Preventative measures should be comprehensive and multi-faceted, including stringent 
enforcement of legal measures to prevent child marriage, raising awareness among adolescents and communities 
about pregnancy-related complications, and providing comprehensive, age-appropriate sexuality education.
Keywords  Adolescent, Pregnancy, Teenage pregnancy, Maternal health, Sexual and reproductive health

review of complications and implications 
of adolescent pregnancy in India
Anjini Vijayan Clitus1, Ali Davod Parsa1, Ibrahim Alasqah2*, Nada Alqarawi2,3, Ilias Mahmud4,5,6 and Russell Kabir1

Introduction
According to the World Health Organization (WHO), 
adolescence spans from 10 to 19  years of age [1]. This 
period is marked by rapid physical, psychological, and 
emotional development as it bridges childhood and 
adulthood. Adolescent pregnancy is a critical public 
health issue in both low- and high-income countries.
Globally, the adolescent birth rate (ABR) has declined, 
but significant variations exist between and within coun­
tries. The global ABR has dropped from 64.5 births per 
1,000 women (aged 15–19) in 2000 to 41.3 births per 
1,000 women in 2023. Despite this decline, the number 
remains high, with WHO estimating 21 million ado­
lescent pregnancies annually in low-income countries, 
resulting in about 12 million births [1]. Factors such as 
child marriage and child sexual abuse contribute to the 
risk of adolescent pregnancy.
Adolescent pregnancy remains a persistent and com­
plex public health issue in India, with significant impli­
cations for the well-being of young mothers and their 
children. India is home to the world's largest adoles­
cent population, comprising approximately 253 million 
young people aged 10–19 years. In rural India alone, 8% 
of women aged 15–19 become pregnant, according to 
the National Family Health Survey-5, underscoring the 
urgent need for evidence-based interventions to address 
adolescent pregnancy in this population [2]. Among 
married adolescents in this age group, over half (55%) 
have already started having children. The prevalence of 
adolescent pregnancy in India is 6.8%, down from 7.9% 
in NFHS-4 (2015–2016) [3]. However, World Bank data 
from 2021 shows an adolescent fertility rate of 17 [4].
Numerous risk factors contribute to adolescent preg­
nancy. In low- and middle-income countries, over 30% of 
girls marry before age 18, and 14% before age 15 [5]. Fac­
tors such as lack of awareness about contraception, low 
literacy rates, and inadequate access to healthcare con­
tribute to India's high rate of adolescent pregnancy [6]. A 
systematic review by Chung, Kim, and Lee (2018) found 
that adolescents with lower socioeconomic status or less 
education are more likely to become pregnant [7].
Adolescent women, with their developing reproduc­
tive systems, are more prone to complications during 
and after pregnancy, leading to serious health problems 
and maternal deaths. Adolescent pregnancy significantly 
impacts both mother and baby, resulting in high mor­
tality and morbidity [8]. It is associated with conditions 
such as preterm birth, low birth weight, perinatal death, 
maternal death, and puerperal sepsis [9].
Studies on complications of adolescent pregnancy have 
shown conflicting results, highlighting the need for a 
comprehensive systematic review in the Indian context 
[6, 7]. Several primary research on adolescent pregnancy 
have been conducted across different regions of India. 
Understanding and addressing these complications is 
crucial for advancing public health initiatives, policy 
reforms, and targeted interventions. This systematic 
review aims to investigate the various maternal, foetal, 
and neonatal complications linked to adolescent preg­
nancy in India.
Methods
Study design
This study is a systematic review of peer-reviewed arti­
cles focused on maternal and foetal complications related 
to adolescent pregnancy in India. The review adhered to 
the PRISMA 2020 guidelines throughout the process.
Research question
The research question was formulated using the PEO 
framework (Population, Exposure, and Outcome): "What 
are the maternal and foetal or neonatal complications in 
adolescent pregnancy in the Indian population?" Table 1 
presents the PEO search tool.
Search strategy
Following the Centre for Reviews and Dissemination 
2009 guidelines, an initial search was conducted in the 
Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews (CDSR) to 
establish the need for this review [10]. After confirming 
no recent systematic reviews, a comprehensive literature 
search was conducted in databases including PubMed, 
Ovid-Embase, Medline, CINAHL Plus, Web of Science, 
and Scopus. Manual searches (reference harvesting) and 
Google Scholar were also used to identify studies not 
indexed in databases. The literature search adhered to 
the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews 
and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA 2020) guidelines (Fig. 1) to 
identify a range of primary studies [11].
A search strategy was developed using relevant key­
words, controlled vocabulary, and MeSH (Medical Sub­
ject Headings) terms. The PEO search strategy tool was 
utilized to identify keywords (Table 1). Boolean operators 
and conjunctions were used to refine search results. The 
search query combined terms such as “adolescence” or 
“teen” or “teenage” and (1) “pregnancy” or “pregnant” or 
“antenatal” or “antenatal mothers” or “perinatal” or “post­
natal” or “post natal mothers” or “adolescent mothers” 
or “adolescent pregnancy” or “maternal” or “foetal” and 
(2) “complications” or “outcomes” or “consequences” or 
“sequelae” and (3) India.

Study selection
The inclusion and exclusion criteria are outlined in 
Table 2. Articles retrieved from databases were imported 
into RefWorks software, and duplicates were removed. A 
manual screening was also performed to prevent dupli­
cation bias. After removing duplicates, the literature 
search yielded 259 papers as shown in Fig. 1. This review 
included only peer-reviewed articles published in Eng­
lish. Two independent reviewers (AVC and RK) con­
ducted all screening phases to ensure consistency and 
minimize selection bias.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria implementation
After removing duplicates, the literature search yielded 
259 papers as shown in Fig. 1. In the first phase, articles 
were screened for study design. In the next stage, titles 
and abstracts were screened using the inclusion and 
exclusion criteria, resulting in 53 articles. Articles lacking 
adequate information were excluded, and 35 articles 
could not be obtained due to unavailability. Ultimately, 
39 articles were chosen for critical appraisal. Disagree­
ments between reviewers at any screening stage were 
resolved through discussion between the two reviewers, 
with consultation from a third reviewer (ADP) when con­
sensus could not be achieved.
Critical appraisal
Critical appraisal was conducted on the 39 articles to 
reduce bias, assess validity, check reliability, and evalu­
ate strengths and weaknesses. Case–control and cohort 
studies were assessed using the Critical Appraisal Skills 
Programme (CASP) (Tables 3 and 4) [37]. Cross-sec­
tional studies were evaluated using the Appraisal tool 
for Cross-sectional Studies (AXIS) (Table 5) [44]. Ethical 
appraisal was also conducted to enhance the ethical and 
methodological quality of the review.
Outcome of critical and ethical appraisal
Critical and ethical appraisal were conducted in two 
sequential phases on the 39 articles retrieved for full-text 
assessment. In the first phase, ethical appraisal assessed 
the presence of ethical approval and documented 
informed consent from study participants. Thirteen 
articles lacked clear ethical approval and/or documented 
informed consent procedures and were excluded at this 
stage, leaving 26 articles for further evaluation. In the 
second phase, the remaining 26 articles underwent criti­
cal appraisal for methodological quality, validity, and rel­
evance using standardized tools (CASP for cohort and 
case–control studies; AXIS for cross-sectional studies). 
Thirteen articles were excluded due to low validity and

relevance, including inadequate methodological quality, 
poor outcome measurement, or outcomes not directly 
addressing the research question. The final 13 articles 
met all criteria for ethical standards, methodological 
quality, validity, and relevance to the research question 
and were retained for the systematic review.
Data abstraction & analysis
Data were extracted using Microsoft Excel by two 
authors, including in-text citation of the article, study 
design, context, duration, inclusion criteria, sample size, 
data source, aim, key findings, and limitations. Data from 
quantitative studies were included in this review. Meta-
analysis was not feasible due to the heterogeneity of out­
come variables and variations in sample sizes among the 
articles, so a narrative synthesis was conducted.
Results
Characteristics of included studies
This systematic review included 13 primary research arti­
cles conducted in various regions of India. Five articles 
specifically focused on obstetric complications [9, 21, 24, 
40, 41], while the remaining articles assessed both mater­
nal and foetal complications and overall outcomes asso­
ciated with adolescent pregnancy.
Designs of included studies
The 13 quantitative studies included in this systematic 
review comprised nine case–control studies, one cohort 
study, and three cross-sectional studies. One study was 
community-based [19], while the others were conducted 
in hospital settings.
Sources of data
Among the 13 articles analysed, five gathered data from 
hospital records [9, 25–27, 34], three utilized interviews 
with pre-structured and pretested questionnaires [19, 29, 
40], and three employed a combination of both methods 
[24, 41, 42]. Notably, two articles did not specify their 
data collection methods [21, 28]. Study characteristics 
such as study design and methods, context, duration, 
inclusion criteria, sample size, data sources, aims, key 
findings, and limitations are presented in Table 6.
Maternal and foetal/neonatal complications
Analysis of the data extracted from the included studies 
reveals both maternal and foetal/neonatal complications 
associated with adolescent pregnancy. Each study mea­
sured different outcomes. The main themes that emerged 
during the analysis are maternal complications during 
the antenatal, intrapartum, and postnatal periods, and 
foetal or newborn complications.

Maternal and foetal/neonatal complications
The incidence of adolescent pregnancy in the included 
studies ranged from 6% to 18.3% [25–27, 34, 41, 42]. 
Analysis of the data extracted from the included studies 
reveals both maternal and foetal/neonatal complications 
associated with adolescent pregnancy. These complica­
tions are organized by timing of occurrence and catego­
rized as follows.
I.	
MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS
Adolescent mothers experienced various maternal com­
plications throughout pregnancy and the postpartum 
period. These complications are categorized by timing of 
occurrence as antenatal (during pregnancy), intrapartum 
(during labour and delivery), and postnatal (after deliv­
ery) complications.
A.	
Antenatal Complications
The majority of adolescent mothers experienced antena­
tal complications. Commonly reported antenatal com­
plications included anaemia, hypertensive disorders of 
pregnancy (including pregnancy-induced hypertension 
and pre-eclampsia), oligohydramnios, gestational diabe­
tes mellitus, preterm labour, and post-datism.
Incidence rates for antenatal complications were sub­
stantial across studies. Dutta and Prasant (2013), Raut 
and Patil (2014), and Mohite et al. (2014) reported ante­
natal complications incidence rates of 68.8%, 65.3%, 
and 48.38%, respectively [21, 28, 41], demonstrating the 
high prevalence of antenatal complications in adolescent 
pregnancies.
Anaemia was the most frequently reported antena­
tal complication in the reviewed articles, with incidence 
rates ranging from 44% to 81.7% across different studies. 
Most studies concluded that anaemia was significantly 
associated with adolescent pregnancy, except Sagili et 
al. (2012), where anaemia was significantly higher in the 
non-adolescent age group [26]. Severe anaemia incidence 
in adolescent pregnancy was reported by Seneesh & Shah 
(2015) and Doddihal & Katti (2017) at 11.4% and 4.1%, 
respectively [19, 29].
Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, including Preg­
nancy Induced Hypertension (PIH) [28, 29, 41, 42] and 
Pre-eclampsia [24, 34], were significantly associated with 
adolescent pregnancy and were reported in multiple 
studies. Although eclampsia was observed in four stud­
ies, it was not found to be statistically significant [24, 27, 
29, 34].
Oligohydramnios was related to adolescent pregnancy 
in three studies [9, 21, 41]. None of the studies found a 
link to gestational diabetes mellitus, antepartum haemor­
rhage, or maternal mortality.
B.	
Intrapartum Complications
Intrapartum complications were documented in sev­
eral studies examining adolescent pregnancies. Specific 
intrapartum complications included non-progression of 
labour, deep transverse arrest [28], perineal tears, and 
episiotomy [26], indicating increased obstetric complex­
ity during labour and delivery.
The majority of adolescent mothers underwent normal 
vaginal delivery, which was statistically significant [9, 21, 
25, 26, 40–42], while the rest underwent either instru­
mental delivery or caesarean section. Only Rudra, Bal, 
& Paul (2013) described emergency caesarean sections, 
which were not statistically significant [25].
Although many authors describe preterm delivery as 
common in adolescent pregnancy, three studies pointed 
to an association with post-datism (prolonged preg­
nancy) [26, 28, 40]. Two articles mentioned significantly 
higher rates of premature rupture of membranes (PROM) 
[25, 42], while Sagili et al. found PROM significantly 
higher in the non-adolescent age group [26]. Sagili et al. 
also did not find a significant relationship with preterm 
delivery in adolescence. However, it should be noted that 
not all included studies described intrapartum complica­
tions comprehensively, limiting the completeness of data 
on this complication category.
C.	
Postnatal Complications
Postpartum complications were described in three stud­
ies included in this review. Postpartum haemorrhage 
was the primary postpartum complication documented. 
However, postpartum haemorrhage was not found to be 
significantly associated with adolescent pregnancy in the 
studies reviewed, suggesting that postpartum bleeding 
complications may not be uniquely elevated in this popu­
lation compared to adult pregnancies.
II.	
FOETAL/NEONATAL COMPLICATION
Complications in the newborn included low birth weight, 
respiratory distress, and subsequent NICU admission. 
Low birth weight was noted in the majority of studies on 
adolescent pregnancy, with two studies reporting signifi­
cant incidences of very low birth weight babies [27].
Preterm delivery resulted in low-birth-weight babies 
among adolescent women, contributing to higher 
NICU admission rates. Five studies demonstrated birth 
asphyxia [21, 28, 40–42].
Intrauterine growth retardation was more frequent in 
adolescent pregnancies compared to the non-adolescent 
age group [21, 25, 28, 41]. Of the six articles mention­

Discussion
This systematic review was conducted to identify the 
impact of adolescent pregnancy on the health of the 
mother and the baby. The review indicated that adoles­
cent women had an elevated risk for complications dur­
ing pregnancy, including both systemic and obstetric 
consequences. While the adolescent pregnancy rate has 
decreased in developed countries, it persists as a public 
health issue in developing countries. In 2021, approxi­
mately 14% of adolescent pregnancies were present glob­
ally, with 10% occurring in South Asia [45].
In India, the wide variation in adolescent pregnancy 
outcomes and reproductive health status across regions is 
attributable to significant disparities in healthcare infra­
structure, availability of reproductive health services, and 
implementation of adolescent-friendly health programs. 
Stillbirth and unsafe abortion are particularly concern­
ing complications in adolescent pregnancies. Adolescents 
face higher risks for unsafe abortion compared to adult 
women due to limited access to safe reproductive health 
services, inadequate reproductive health knowledge, 
and significant social and economic barriers [23]. These 
adverse outcomes can affect adolescent mothers physi­
cally and emotionally during their critical developmental 
transition from childhood to adulthood, with potential 
for long-term health and psychological consequences. 
The adverse outcomes of adolescent pregnancy in India 
can also be attributed to low socioeconomic backgrounds 
and child marriage. According to a study conducted by 
Shri et al. (2023) in Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, 61% of mar­
ried adolescent women became pregnant before age 20, 
with 18.57% experiencing miscarriage, abortion, or still­
birth [46]. Adolescent pregnancy is influenced by early 
marriage and sexual abuse. Pregnant adolescents are less 
likely to seek proper antenatal care, which contributes to 
complications in this age group [47]. Low socioeconomic 
status, large family size, and higher birth order also affect 
the outcomes of adolescent pregnancy. A multi-country 
study in Sub-Saharan Africa by Mombo-Ngomo et al. 
(2016) categorizes adolescent mothers into a high-risk 
group, suggesting that routine care may not be sufficient 
to avoid complications [48].
Differences in the studies can be attributed to the 
services mothers receive, study area (urban or rural), 
and study duration [41]. From the thirteen included 
studies, anaemia, preterm delivery, and low birth weight 
were consistent complications associated with adoles­
cent pregnancy. Devi et al. (2019) found that 86% of 
the adolescent age group were primigravida and that 
anaemia was not due to young age but nutritional defi­
ciencies, malaria, and hookworm infestation [9]. Adam 
and Ali (2016) commented that anaemia in pregnancy 
is due to physiological changes leading to haemodilu­
tion and increased iron requirements [49]. Sagili et al. 
(2012) found that anaemia was significantly higher in 
non-adolescents, likely due to early iron supplement 
intake in booked cases [26]. Although hypertensive dis­
orders of pregnancy were significantly associated with 
adolescent pregnancy, no study found a relation with 
eclampsia. Only Devi et al. (2019) demonstrated signifi­
cant oligohydramnios in adolescent mothers, attributing 
the increased caesarean section rate to immature birth 
canals and pelvic bones [9]. Preterm delivery resulted in 
low-birth-weight babies among adolescent women, con­
tributing to higher NICU admission rates. Pre-eclamp­
sia, anaemia, and poor nutritional status are the main 
causes of premature and low-birth-weight newborns 
[21]. Raut and Patil (2014) conducted a prospective case–
control study comparing 160 adolescent mothers (aged 
13–19 years, mean age 15.8 years) with 160 adult control 
mothers (aged 20–25 years). The study revealed that ado­
lescents experienced significantly higher rates of antena­
tal complications (65.3% vs. 34.7% in the adult group). 
Specifically, adolescents demonstrated substantially ele­
vated rates of cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD) (100% 
vs. 0% in adults), intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) 
(83.3% vs. 16.7% in adults), and anaemia (75% vs. 25% 
in adults). Additionally, adverse foetal outcomes were 
markedly elevated in the adolescent group, with 70% of 
caesarean sections, 76% of preterm deliveries, and 56% 
of low birth weight babies occurring in the adolescent 
mothers compared to 30%, 24%, and 44% respectively in 
the adult control group [28]. There are marked variations 
in reporting caesarean and vaginal delivery rates among 
adolescents. Raut and Patil (2014) also concluded that 
CPD contributed to increased operative interventions 
(70.8%) and instrumental deliveries (61%) [28]. In con­
trast, Medhi et al. (2016) and Seneesh and Shah (2015) 
found no increase in caesarean sections among adoles­
cents but reported increased vaginal deliveries, which 
can be explained by the low birth weight and preterm 
delivery of the babies [24, 29]. Most studies concluded 
that preterm delivery was more frequent in adolescent 
mothers, though Sagili et al. (2012) reported signifi­
cant post-datism in this age group [26]. Mukhopadhyay, 
Chaudhuri, and Paul (2010) found that early pregnancy 
registration and iron and folic acid tablet consumption 
were lower in adolescent pregnancies, with a higher pro­
portion of stillbirths [40]. Adolescent pregnancy was not

Since most studies were conducted in hospital set­
tings, the outcomes might differ for the general popula­
tion. It was identified that complications in this age group 
could be significantly reduced where adequate antenatal 
care and regular check-ups are provided. As this review 
highlighted, pregnancy can adversely affect the health of 
adolescents. In the reviewed articles describing anaemia, 
the type of anaemia was not specified. Preterm deliv­
ery of newborns has consequences such as IUGR, birth 
asphyxia due to respiratory distress syndrome, and reti­
nopathy of prematurity.
This review has some strengths and limitations. It iden­
tified adverse foetal, neonatal, and maternal outcomes of 
adolescent pregnancy in the Indian population through 
a comprehensive search using PRISMA 2020 guidelines, 
helping reduce bias from multiple individual studies and 
providing evidence-based data for more reliable and 
accurate conclusions. The review identified gaps in exist­
ing literature. However, it only included peer-reviewed 
articles, which is both a strength and a limitation. Addi­
tionally, articles where full text could not be retrieved 
were excluded, potentially omitting up-to-date research. 
The review was conducted by a single author without 
cross-checking, which might have affected the quality of 
reporting and analysis. This review also did not consider 
the socio-demographic background of adolescents, lead­
ing to potential confounding bias. Some included studies 
had small sample sizes. Meta-analysis was not performed 
due to a lack of reported estimated effect sizes, limiting 
the review's statistical power. This review did not explore 
the role of dietary habits, intimate partner violence, and 
socioeconomic profile in leading to complications of ado­
lescent pregnancy.
Conclusions
In conclusion, adolescent pregnancy in India represents 
a complex public health challenge with significant con­
sequences for maternal and neonatal health, as well as 
broader societal implications. Pregnancy-related compli­
cations, including anaemia and hypertensive disorders, 
contribute to increased maternal morbidity and may 
disrupt educational attainment and economic opportu­
nities, thereby perpetuating cycles of poverty. Preterm 
birth and low birth weight in infants born to adolescent 
mothers result in higher neonatal morbidity requiring 
intensive care, with potential long-term developmen­
tal and health sequelae. These complications collectively

impose substantial economic burdens on healthcare sys­

Multi-level interventions are necessary to address 
adolescent pregnancy effectively. At the policy level, 
strengthening and enforcing legislation to prevent child 
marriage is critical, complemented by development and 
scaling of adolescent-friendly health services. Prevention-
focused strategies should prioritize comprehensive, age-
appropriate sexuality education integrated into school 
curricula, coupled with improved access to contraceptive 
information, counselling, and methods through health­
care facilities. Healthcare systems must ensure acces­
sible, quality antenatal care including early pregnancy 
registration, regular monitoring, nutritional support, 
and targeted counselling for pregnant adolescents. Public 
health campaigns, tailored to both rural and urban con­
texts, should communicate the documented health risks 
and complications associated with adolescent pregnancy, 
engaging schools, healthcare facilities, community lead­
ers, and other stakeholders as implementation partners. 
Psychosocial support systems should be strengthened to 
assist both adolescent mothers and their families in man­
aging the physical, emotional, and social challenges asso­
ciated with early parenthood.
Addressing adolescent pregnancy requires coordinated 
action across health, education, social protection, and 
policy sectors. Given the documented burden of mater­
nal and neonatal complications, reducing adolescent 
pregnancy should remain a priority area in India's public 
health agenda, supported by adequate resources, trained 
personnel, and systematic monitoring of implementation 
outcomes.
